Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Shift 4, green bar at 25 pixels (~5 actions left). Yellow block at position 9, coords (37, 48).
- Goal model: Maybe I need to click on the yellow block (unique color in perimeter).
- Plan: Click on the yellow block at (37, 48).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 57 actions; a strong score needs about 17 or fewer. You are 3.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! I see there are small yellow blocks (4 pixels each) at positions (17, 10), (17, 16), (23, 10), and (23, 16). These are in the top-left corner, outside the perimeter rectangle. They seem to be part of a pattern.
Also, I see there's a charcoal block (8 pixels) at (19, 16) and (23, 12).
Let me look at the top-left corner more carefully:
- (17, 10): Y, 4 pixels
- (17, 16): Y, 4 pixels
- (19, 12): w, 16 pixels (this is part of the perimeter)
- (19, 16): c, 8 pixels
- (19, 18): g, 16 pixels (this is part of the perimeter)
- (23, 10): Y, 4 pixels
- (23, 12): c, 8 pixels
- (23, 16): Y, 4 pixels
So there's a pattern of yellow blocks at (17, 10), (17, 16), (23, 10), (23, 16). These form a 2x2 grid with gaps. And there are charcoal blocks at (19, 16) and (23, 12).
Wait, let me look at the image again. The top-left corner has:
- Row 17: Y at col 10, Y at col 16
- Row 19: w (perimeter) at col 12, c at col 16, g (perimeter) at col 18
- Row 23: Y at col 10, c at col 12, Y at col 16
So the pattern is:
Y . . . . Y
. w c . . .
. . . . . .
Y c . . Y
Hmm, this is interesting. The yellow blocks form a sort of frame, and the charcoal blocks are inside.
Wait, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me look at the image more carefully.
Looking at the image, I see:
- Top row of the "inner" pattern: Y, Y (two yellow blocks)
- Second row: w (light gray, perimeter), c (charcoal)
- Third row: Y (yellow block)
- Fourth row: Y, c, Y
Actually, I think the yellow blocks at (17, 10), (17, 16), (23, 10), (23, 16) are NOT part of the perimeter. They're separate objects. And the charcoal blocks at (19, 16) and (23, 12) are also separate.
Let me think about what these objects might represent. Maybe they're "targets" or "goals" that I need to match with the perimeter colors.
Actually, I just realized something. The charcoal blocks (c) are at (19, 16) and (23, 12). These are BETWEEN the perimeter blocks. Maybe they're "gaps" or "missing" blocks in the perimeter.
Wait, let me reconsider. The perimeter is a rectangle of 20 blocks. The blocks at (19, 12), (19, 18), (19, 24), etc. are the perimeter blocks. The blocks at (19, 16) and (23, 12) are NOT part of the perimeter; they're inside the rectangle.
Actually, looking at the coordinates:
- (19, 12): w, perimeter block (position 0)
- (19, 16): c, 8 pixels (not a perimeter block)
- (19, 18): g, perimeter block (position 1)
- (23, 10): Y, 4 pixels (not a perimeter block)
- (23, 12): c, 8 pixels (not a perimeter block)
- (23, 16): Y, 4 pixels (not a perimeter block)
So the non-perimeter blocks are:
- (17, 10): Y, 4 pixels
- (17, 16): Y, 4 pixels
- (19, 16): c, 8 pixels
- (23, 10): Y, 4 pixels
- (23, 12): c, 8 pixels
- (23, 16): Y, 4 pixels
These form a pattern in the top-left corner. Let me see if clicking on any of these does something.
Let me try clicking on one of the yellow blocks, say (17, 10).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Shift 4. There are small yellow and charcoal blocks in the top-left corner that I haven't tried clicking on.
Goal model: Maybe I need to click on these small blocks in the top-left corner.
Plan: Try clicking on the yellow block at (17, 10).
